best_image_source: Esri World Imagery aerial photo dated 11/13/2025

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Northwest upper block shows repeated rigid rectangular panel fields with regular linear seams and a panelized, reflective grid pattern consistent with metal roofing; similar panelized bright sections appear on other attached upper blocks.

Large central and eastern roof fields appear as broad smooth white low-slope membrane-like surfaces with few resolved seams, consistent with a white single-ply roof; TPO is the conservative default for an unresolved white membrane-like field.
The same smooth bright membrane-like fields could also be PVC because aerial imagery does not show product-specific markers and the surface is uniformly light-colored.
Some bright low-slope areas could represent a reflective coating over an existing roof, but coating-specific application variation, wear-through, or exposed substrate are not clearly resolved.

roof_age_estimate: Unknown from aerial imagery; overall appears to be a combination of newer-looking bright membrane fields and distinct panelized roof sections, but exact installation dates cannot be confirmed.
roof_pitch: Predominantly low-slope with some steeper/panelized roof planes

notes: Several white roof areas show tan/brown discoloration and irregular darker patches near transitions and lower roof edges, which may indicate staining, recurring ponding, or prior repair/water-damage potential.
A few roof fields show localized uneven coloration and darker edge areas; image resolution does not allow confirmation of whether these are shadows or moisture-related marks.
Rooftop penetrations/equipment are numerous across the low-slope fields, increasing flashing and leak potential.
Trees and landscaped areas are close to parts of the building perimeter; some overhang and adjacent debris/shadow risk are visible near edges.

observations: Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, possible ponding or drainage stress, high penetration or rooftop-unit density, tree overhang or roof debris exposure.
The building is clearly mixed-roof based on visible panelized sections and broad white membrane-like roof fields.
Northwest upper roof blocks show strong metal cues: rigid panel geometry, repeated seams, and directional reflectance.
Most large roof fields read as bright smooth low-slope membrane surfaces, but exact chemistry cannot be confirmed from aerial imagery alone.
Multiple rooftop units, vents, and curbs are distributed across the roof, creating many flashing points.
summary: Esri World Imagery dated 11/13/2025 shows a mixed-roof commercial school with distinct panelized metal roof sections and extensive bright low-slope membrane areas. Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, possible ponding or drainage stress, high penetration or rooftop-unit density, tree overhang or roof debris exposure.
recommendation: Recommend a qualified commercial roofing contractor perform an onsite inspection to confirm the visible roof systems, test for moisture where the stained or darker areas warrant it, and document flashing, drainage, and penetration conditions. For the apparently restorable low-slope membrane areas, also evaluate a silicone roof restoration system before considering a more costly overlay or replacement; a qualifying silicone system can potentially reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, withstand ponding water when properly designed and installed, improve resistance to UV. A qualified commercial roof-coating contractor should perform an onsite inspection, complete moisture testing when warranted, verify drainage, moisture, penetrations, flashing, and debris-related damage, confirm silicone-system and warranty eligibility, and provide a reliable project-price proposal.

roof_zones.location: northwest upper block and adjacent upper-left roof fields
supporting_cues: More reflective, panelized appearance than adjacent membrane areas
limitations: Aerial-only view limits confirmation of exact metal subtype and attachment method
Some adjacent bright surfaces could be skylights or other rooftop components

roof_zones.location: central and eastern main roof masses with bright white low-slope fields
supporting_cues: Large contiguous smooth white membrane-like fields
Few resolved seams at aerial resolution
Broad low-slope roof planes consistent with single-ply roofing
limitations: TPO cannot be separated from PVC or coating with high confidence from this imagery alone
Rooftop equipment, shadows, and parapets limit flashing visibility

roof_zones.location: south-central connecting roof and lower-left/southwest attached section
supporting_cues: Surface character consistent with dominant white membrane areas
Distinct from the panelized metal sections
limitations: Small geometry changes and shadows make the boundary harder to resolve
No clear membrane seam pattern is visible at this scale

roof_zones.location: northwest upper block and adjacent upper-left roof fields

candidates.evidence: Two large rectangular roof areas show a regular ribbed/panel grid with repeated linear seams and a more rigid, reflective module pattern than the surrounding roofs.
Light-colored low-slope surfaces are present, but the grid-like pattern is more consistent with metal or translucent panel assemblies than a smooth membrane.
Resolution is limited for distinguishing whether all visible panelized fields are metal roof, skylights, or another roof assembly.

limitations: Some adjacent bright surfaces may be skylights or roof-mounted panels rather than roof field.
Subtype cannot be confirmed from the aerial view alone.

roof_zones.location: central and eastern main roof masses with bright white low-slope fields

candidates.evidence: Large contiguous white, smooth-looking low-slope roof areas read as single-ply membrane fields; seams are not clearly resolved, so TPO is the conservative default.
The roof is a uniform white membrane-like surface, but there is no specific PVC-only evidence beyond appearance.
Some bright areas could represent coated surfaces, but coating-specific application variation is not clearly visible.

limitations: White membrane chemistry cannot be separated reliably from color alone.
Rooftop equipment, shadows, and parapets limit visibility of laps and flashing details.

roof_zones.location: south-central connecting roof and lower-left/southwest attached section

candidates.evidence: These attached low-slope fields are also bright and smooth-looking, consistent with the dominant white membrane roof areas.
Uniform light-colored membrane-like appearance could fit PVC, but no specific indicators are visible.
A reflective finish is possible, though there is insufficient evidence of coating-specific patching or substrate show-through.

limitations: Small geometry changes and shadows make the material transition hard to separate from the adjacent roof zones.
No clear membrane seam pattern is resolved at this scale.

overall_limitations: Aerial resolution is insufficient to distinguish TPO from PVC or coating on the bright membrane areas with high confidence.
Several rooftop elements may be equipment screens, skylights, or panels and should not be treated as roof-field material without clearer evidence.
Area percentages are approximate and based on visible roof extent rather than precise measurement.
